activity_title: ROUTINE HEALTH VISIT for child WITHOUT significant complex, chronic, or special health care needs
instrument_title: Providing preventative primary care for children of all ages
feedback_value: ****** develops beautiful rapport with families, and she has such a wonderful way of counseling families. She is clear, thoughtful, and deliberate. She puts things in patient-centered language. What she is working on now (and she is even a bit ahead of where she should be for her level of training in this) is continuing to build her scripts for different issues commonly encountered in primary care. Here, through our conversations, she developed more of a script for navigating picky eating, toddler discipline, etc. She delivers the message beautifully and in her own words. She also puts them nicely in her note in an organized manner that would help a future provider understand the context of the discussion. Keep up the fantastic work!
supervision_anchor: SUPERVISOR AND RESIDENT CO-LED CARE: Required supervising physician(s) to direct some aspects of the care

activity_title: Single Free Text Question- GENERAL FEEDBACK
feedback_value: Independently seeking out resources to understand medical decision making and to generate higher-level discussion with the subpsecialty team, without prompting and with enthusiasm. Pro-active in making new connections between concepts from one patient encounter to the next, or a teaching session and a new consult. Willingness to participate in pathophysiology and other discussions, at a high level, despite this being the first three days on a rotation. She asked thought-provoking questions in our division-wide case conference that highlighted this curiosity, rapid mastery of new material, and intellectual curiosity. Engaging and empathetic questions and reflections during patient/family interactions that ensure the family knows their concerns are being heard, their distress is acknowledged, and/or the plan is well understood. Willingness to try new approaches to her framework for patient data collection/presentation/understanding in the setting of a new subspecialty, and ability to integrate suggestions in real time, demonstrating a flexible mindset and growth mindset.
next_level_value: As you progress as a resident, in addition to the data collection and interpretation leading to a diagnosis, layer in a pathophysiologic model for their presentation and the expected/unexpected response to interventions. While listed as a suggestion, you immediately incorporated this into patient presentations/encounters e.g. assessing the physiology of the thyroiditis patient rather than describing the abnormal TFT, e.g. evaluating the urine osm of AVP deficiency patient with AKI in response to DDAVP rather than reporting the Uosm value. Impressive!

feedback_value: ***** did a lot of spontaneous teaching for our medical student and even the MTAs on rounds , explaining things about patients they may have missed and really setting the medical student up for success on family centered rounds. I can see her passion for teaching and she is great at it! I'm so glad she is taking the initiative to help the other learners on the team and I know it makes the rotation so much better for them.

commitment_to_growth_and_learning_value: *** did a really good job of seeking out feedback from me throughout his rotation. In addition to asking me specifically for feedback, he was interested in improving his assessments and his notes. And he incorporated my feedback into his notes subsequent days.

activity_title: Single Free Text Question- COMMUNICATING WITH PATIENT AND FAMILIES/PROVIDING FAMILY-CENTERED CARE
assessment_type_anchor: Based on a single encounter or day
family_centered_care_value: I have been extremely impressed with ********'s ability to own the room as the primary provider in the Nursery and navigate some challenging and varied discussions with stressed and distraught families. In this instance, a baby had severe weight loss and needed to start on phototherapy for jaundice and I prompted ******** to remain the primary communicator with this family throughout the day to provide updates and negotiate Shared decision-making on feeding plans and phototherapy plans. She did this in a way that was direct yet brought empathy and flexibility with the family. She was an empathic listener and patient when the parent was feeling emotional and stressed. And she was able to provide smart options for the family to choose from in terms of management that led to success in getting the infants' weight and bilirubin on an improved trajectory to achieve timely discharge.

activity_title: Single Free Text Question- GENERAL FEEDBACK
feedback_value: ***** has an amazing attitude is always wanting to improve. I'm really impressed with how well she takes constructive feedback and works on those areas. I've seen growth from when I was on with her last - presentations have improved and she seems to be much more comfortable talking with families in patient-centered rounds.
next_level_value: I talked with ***** that it would be good to further develop her assessments and plan. I liked the way she discussed with one of our patients the different areas we were focusing on - in this case differentiating the broader work-up from some of the feeding based issues.

activity_title: ACUTE COMMON PROBLEM
feedback_value: **** took care of this patient while simultaneously supervising a third year medical student. I think she did a great job providing autonomy to the student allowing him to do the history and exam under her supervision, as well as allowing him to present the case. When we were doing teaching about the management of asthma, **** did a great job of stepping in and pulling out a clinical practice guideline and using this to teach the student about assessment of level of illness and how this modifies our approach to the treatment plan. Her assessment of the patient and recommended treatment plan were spot on, she did serial reassessments With an appropriate adjustment of his care and did nice work independently coming up with a disposition plan and prescribing him an inhaler for home. Particularly notable was that she found a video instructing the family on the use of a spacer in their preferred language of Haitian Creole without being prompted. This really demonstrated an impressive attention to detail and equitable approach to healthcare. My only recommendation, which was provided at the time we cared for this patient was to make sure we enter the room with an age appropriate and broad differential diagnosis considering various things that can cause wheezing (eg foreign body aspiration) and making sure that when teaching a student, we encourage them to consider these different causes while obtaining the history and focusing their physical exam.
supervision_anchor: RESIDENT LED CARE WITHOUT SUPERVISION NEEDED: May benefit from continued learning and growth; however, care would have been safe and effective without a supervisor

activity_title: Single Free Text Question- GENERAL FEEDBACK
feedback_value: Welcome to the EDC Dr. ********! The ED can be a hard place to start for new interns given the rapid paced environment. However, Dr. ******** did not let that stop him! He was an integral part of the team. He quickly picked up patients and did accurate history and examinations. He was a model adult learner. He sought to understand each patient and started to develop pattern recognition for common pediatric complaints. When his thoughts about the patient differed from the attendings, he sought to understand why so that he could learn from each experience. Next steps for Dr. ******** is to expand his pattern recognition by seeing variations of the most common chief complaints.
next_level_value: See prior.

activity_title: ROUTINE HEALTH VISIT for child WITH complex, chronic, or special health care needs
instrument_title: Provide comprehensive healthcare for a child with complex, chronic, or special health care needs
feedback_value: ***** saw two complex psychosocial patients of mine for a well visit where behavior and mental health concerns were quite significant. There are multiple SDOH-related barriers to care. ***** took a detailed history and presented it efficiently. She incorporated the complex social concerns into her assessment and plan. While she was not sure of the logistical ways to help the family/patients, she provided a clear assessment and plan first before our clinical discussion. For improvement, ***** can focus on being more succinct in history-gathering. While the history was complicated, the plan was somewhat straightforward (school supports, therapy and involving social work), so for future patients, can focus on the most pertinent historical points (of what has been done in the past), including barriers, which can guide the plan. This family has a lot going on, so balance parental concerns with next steps in future encounters (since a lot of the minutia we will not be able to fix or address today). It would also be appropriate in a more busy clinic day to get brief highlights and have the family return for another visit to discuss in more detail (so there is time to get a full history) and/or to consider a telehealth visit if access/travsportation/time barriers exist.
supervision_anchor: SUPERVISOR LED CARE: Required supervising physician(s) to direct the majority of the care

activity_title: ROUTINE HEALTH VISIT for child WITHOUT significant complex, chronic, or special health care needs
instrument_title: Providing preventative primary care for children of all ages
feedback_value: ****** did a thorough job in discussing and following up with parents with vaccine hesitancy, understanding concerns and navigating differing viewpoints from mom and dad. This is a family with whom ****** has been designated the PCP and important relationship building and trust was established. This visit also needed to address a variety of issues beyond vaccine hesitancy including feedings, switching formulas, stooling, etc. ****** was able to identify the varied issues and responded well to minor suggestions about how to differentiate between "normal" and "abnormal" and how to communicate this effectively back to the family while simultaneously acknowledging all the concerns. When a visit has multiple concerns and problems to address, one way is to prioritize what the family feels is the most important concern to them, understand why, make sure to address that issue, and if appropriate, continue further discussion in subsequent visits.

feedback_value: You did a fantastic job navigating some important consults. You articulated the questions and complexities of the situations clearly which yielded helpful responses from both consulting teams. The endocrine situation was complicated given that information that we received ended up being slightly different between members of their team, but there was nothing to have done differently in that situation on our end, as the questions were very clearly addressed. For the G.I. situation, to take it to the next level, could further probe what would be considered abnormal such that we could then give the family clear return precautions. From my end, it is also important that they document that these findings are normal which is less directly about patient care and more for medical legal ramifications. That piece is my responsibility to ensure that it is appropriately done and so again, nothing differently to have done here.

activity_title: Single Free Text Question- GENERAL FEEDBACK
feedback_value: I had the pleasure of working with ***** during her two week NICU rotation. This was *****'s first time being in a NICU and she did a nice job jumping in. The census was not high during our block together, but ***** did carry one of the more active surgical babies in addition to other babies. ***** started out with nice clear goals she wanted to work on during her two weeks. One of her goals was to understand the why behind screening tests and management plans. For example, why do we get HUS studies on certain days of life. ***** would ask questions on rounds or after if something wasn't clear and I appreciated that. I could see her thinking and trying to understand why we choose the management plans we do. On once occasion I was explaining my thought process on how we would start feeding a preemie with a newly placed g tube. ***** had questions about this feeding plan and made sure to let me know that she needed further clarification from me. I think that's a lovely strength to be able to say that something doesn't make sense and to ask for clarification. In our end of the rotation feedback, I urged ***** to continue to ask questions on rounds to gain understanding as to why we do the things we do. These discussions are invaluable for the entire team. Another area to continue to grow upon is setting up the bed during deliveries.
next_level_value: Please see text

activity_title: Single Free Text Question- COMMUNICATING WITH PATIENT AND FAMILIES/PROVIDING FAMILY-CENTERED CARE
family_centered_care_value: ***** did a fantastic job communicating with the families he was taking care of. Updating them regularly and making them feel heard. He explained GMH to a family, printed out a picture to help them understand and also tried to hear their worries and made them feel less anxious. I would encourage him to continue to expand on his assessments and differentials for learning while tailoring his explanation of the most relevant information to families.

activity_title: ACUTE COMMON PROBLEM
feedback_value: In this encounter, I appreciated that ******* asked specific, pertinent questions to clarify parts of the history during rounds while remaining open to additional questions by myself and the supervising resident to add to that history. One thing ******* can continue to work on is trying to use more narrow and specific language when talking about the diagnoses with patients - for example, with this patient, being able to share and explain to the patient that there are many different causes of vertigo, some of which have been ruled out, and some of which we were still working to assess and treat. Being able to express uncertainty about the final diagnosis while also describe how we have ruled some out can be a helpful way to get the patient to understand their overall disease processes. Another important thing I always think about as the attending is what is limiting the ability to safely transition home, and in this patient's case that included her level of disability- and one thing I tried to role model was getting a more direct assessment of that level of disability, in this case, having the patient walk in the room for us. That piece of information ultimately informed our decision to consult PT for safety clearance, which ultimately led to a more specific diagnosis of her vertigo. ******* can keep that question in mind - both what are the things we have to do as the physician team to get a patient out the door but also what symptoms or constraints are limiting the patient from getting home safely?

family_centered_care_value: *** was taken care of an adolescent with chronic leg pain. This patient has been in more or less intractable pain for months and this is their second admission in the past month to our hospital. We had a tough conversation with the patient around how we need to focus more and rehabilitation over more meds and that left the patient in tears and stating she wanted to die. *** spent over an hour with this patient and also her mother (who came about 30 minutes after the convo began) and was able to transform a very upsetting situation into one where the patient was calm and able to engage in life a bit again. It was very impressive.

activity_title: Single Free Text Question- COMMUNICATING WITH PATIENT AND FAMILIES/PROVIDING FAMILY-CENTERED CARE
family_centered_care_value: ***** makes communicating with families a priority in his day. He was very committed to including families in rounds in the NICU. Over the few days that we worked together at the start of his NICU rotation, I observed growth in his ability to communicate clearly with families. To enhance his communication skills further, I would encourage ***** to use simple, clear language and aim to be concise and direct. Sometimes as physicians we talk too much or try to provide a long pathophysiology explanation when all the family wants to know is if this condition is treatable, so including silence and opportunities to listen to the family speak and share their concerns are equally as important as us talking. Long explanations can often be more confusing for families than simple, direct statements followed by silence for them to process the information and ask questions.

feedback_value: ***** did a great job taking on head of bed for a preterm infant born at 32 weeks with multiple risk factors for needing resuscitation. She was able to perform the initial steps of stabilization (warm, dry, stimulate). The fellow identified the need for PPV due to apnea and initiated it, then when infant responded well ***** was able to step in as head of bed again and perform CPAP effectively to keep the infant stabilized for transport. Overall appropriate skills for a resident in the delivery room. With additional experience she will be able to even more quickly identify the need for more advanced resuscitation steps like PPV. Great job being willing to take on a more complex patient than the standard term/near term infant!

We also could anticipate some of the admissions, so she could potentially utilize the signout time to prep notes; and/or use the EMR for her note-taking as opposed to writing things down that later has to be transcribed. activity_title: Single Free Text Question- GENERAL FEEDBACK
assessment_type_anchor: Based on a single encounter or day
feedback_value: **** did a fantastic job navigating a very complex situation. This was a newborn who needed a blood draw to recheck bili. In addition, while we were seeing the patient, we got a call saying that the baby's newborn screen had come back with positive results and needed to be repeated. **** explained this to the mother very patiently and clearly. When she had questions, he addressed them in a very detailed and attentive manner. His demeanor was earnest and kind. I was impressed by the way he was able to calmly deliver this complicated information in a very empathetic and reassuring way. When the patient went to the lab, the tech was unable to get the blood after many attempts, and the mother initially left very frustrated. **** and I stayed late after clinic hours to call the mother back and work with her until she was comfortable coming back for another attempt at the main campus lab. They successfully obtained the blood and **** called her that night to discuss the results. He went over and above to provide the best care to this patient and family! Thanks **** for your great work!
next_level_value: I really like the way you listen so carefully to the family and address their specific concerns in a very calm and reassuring manner. Taking time to truly listen to a family and hear everything they want to say is extremely important in primary care. This situation became difficult after the parent left, but we were able to navigate it. Validating the mother's feelings with the blood draw and all your work in the room earning her trust ultimately brought her back.

activity_title: Single Free Text Question- GENERAL FEEDBACK
assessment_type_anchor: Based on a single encounter or day
feedback_value: *********'s goal this shift was to work on balancing care priorities while carrying an ED patient load - and she did great! She appropriately dropped everything to see a patient in status epilepticus and remained active in that patients care throughout ongoing brief seizures and eventual intubation, coordinating care with neuro and the PICU among other roles. Simultaneously, she was able to get a psych consult rolling for a patient with severe depression prior to staffing. She was then able to see and plan for a lower acuity patient while the attending was wrapping up in the trauma room. For high volume day shifts when the chaos can feel overwhelming, remember 1. You are usually part of a bigger team, so don't need to feel responsible for every patient; and 2. Run your list frequently and assess what needs to happen next to move forward patient care. I'm sure as she gets used to the ED again ********* will have no trouble balancing large patient volumes!

family_centered_care_value: ****** did such a lovely job interacting with a patient with ASD and catatonia. She gets down to his eye level, offers his augmentive communication devices, and always warns him when she is about to do anything. She is so deeply caring and patient with all patients. I am continually impressed with her thoughtfulness and holistic view of these complex patients and think she has a natural affinity for providing trauma informed care.

activity_title: Single Free Text Question- GENERAL FEEDBACK
feedback_value: **** was a delight to have as the supervising resident on the hematology service. She was consistent thoughtful, always thought about all the issues that we could face as we rounded on patients (e.g. could there be challenges with discharge, etc.), was great about updating us throughout the day on changes to the inpatient service, and was a terrific teacher for a new intern who just started in the ***. **** is an outstanding resident with incredible potential. Hopefully she will consider hematology.
next_level_value: Continue to grow as an outstanding clinician.

activity_title: Single Free Text Question- HIGH-VALUE CARE
providing_high_value_care_value: One thing I'd encourage ***** to continue to think about proactively for each of her patients is whether or not an intervention is really going to improve the quality of care being provided, what she will do with the information received from the test, and what potential consequences there might be of getting the test if it is not completely necessary. There were a few times this week where I encouraged ***** to consider NOT getting labs on a patient, for example, not repeating a CBC and retic on a patient with sickle cell dz without a change in clinical status, not repeating a BMP on a patient whose fluid status clinically had improved and who had no other reason to continue to have metabolic derangements outside acute dehydration, or considering getting a hematocrit and retic count instead of a full CBC on a newborn with hyperbilirubinemia to avoid false positive WBC results. These seemingly miniature value-based decisions can really add up when they are multiplied over someone's practice over time and thousands of patients... And of course, always avoiding a needle Stick is helpful on Pediatrics in general!

activity_title: Single Free Text Question- GENERAL FEEDBACK
feedback_value: Dr. **** did a tremendous job on EDC! Faculty comments on high numbers of unsolicited complements around her communication. She practiced detail oriented medicine all while caring for a high number of patients. She did not miss a single task. Dr. **** is performing beyond what we would expect for her level of training. Specific examples include coordination of multiple consultants on complex patients. Frequently at her level, residents may take consultant recommendations as a "to do" list rather than an open conversation. Dr. **** did not do this, always making sure she understood and directed her patients' care. Another example is her flexibility to change the course of care on a patient in which she received a signout. This is a difficult task for even our junior fellows! Faculty comment that next steps for Dr. **** will be increased exposure and familiarity to surgical disease. This is not uncommon for our *** residents. That being said, one faculty member noted a trauma activation in which Dr. **** did an excellent primary and secondary survey! So, she is clearly on her way.
next_level_value: See above.

activity_title: Single Free Text Question- COMMUNICATING WITH PATIENT AND FAMILIES/PROVIDING FAMILY-CENTERED CARE
family_centered_care_value: I observed *****'s communication with families during multiple prenatal consultations, one of which was for a complex infant with several congenital anomalies in addition to prematurity. I appreciated that she was eager to take the lead even in a more complex situation. She did a great job conveying information in a concise way without using a lot to medical jargon. I gave her a few tips on specific language I like to use (e.g. 'breathing' instead of 'respiratory', 'what have you heard' instead of 'what do you understand') and she was very receptive to feedback. She did a good job starting the conversation by introducing herself; one recommendation I have is to also take a few minutes to explain your purpose (e.g. 'Your obstetric team asked us to come speak with you about what to expect if your baby is born in the next few days', or for example if giving an update for a baby in Triage 'I'm the physician caring for your infant in the NICU and I wanted to give you an update.'), then spend a few minutes building rapport by asking about their pregnancy so far or what brought them into the hospital, if it's a boy or girl, if they picked a name if it has special meaning to them, etc; adding a few "social" questions like this at the start might help make the conversation feel more natural. She did a good job responding to parents' questions and focusing on what was importantly to them.

activity_anchor: Supervise others (e.g., intern, medical student)
feedback_value: As PHM senior, ****** did a really nice job leading a group of three interns who were at different levels of confidence and needed different levels of supervision. She had two interns and one junior resident who had transferred in from another program. The one intern who had the least experience with family centered Rounds improved over the first six days of her PHM rotation. By the end of the six days she was more comfortable with rounds and her presentations were clearer for both the families and the medical team. A second intern required a little bit more oversight due to some overconfidence and desire to function more independently. The junior resident needed only gentle guidance. ****** was able to navigate these three residents and their differing needs quite well. All 3 had growth during the week. ******'s ability to model organization, efficiency, task oriented behaviors, and family centered language helped all of the residents improve. ****** is going to work on incorporating teaching into Rounds. With a large team and 3 residents teaching during rounds is complicated because the residents are focusing on their individual tasks and may need to be brought together before she can teach the whole team based on the previous patient encounter.

feedback_value: I've worked with ***** throughout the entirety of her academic development block. ***** has done an absolutely fantastic job working on a solid tumor clinical liquid biopsy study. She had presented the preliminary findings of this study at multiple conferences over the last year and spent academic development block putting it together into a manuscript. She has done a ********* job developing the figures and writing the text for the manuscript and compiling feedback from the co-authors. She has been very responsive met all of her deadlines and very, very thoughtful and how she is compiled feedback. I would encourage ***** to continue to be proactive in her research ********. I would also encourage her to take some risks in terms of looking at the data and new ways reading other related studies and thinking about how other authors might have have approached specific research questions. *******, ***** did a fantastic job and I would continue to encourage her to keep up the good work.
next_level_value: My feedback is focused on *****'s research during her academic development block so I don't have specific feedback on how she should be a better clinician. In terms of improving her research skills I would encourage her to continue to seek out new projects continue to read related manuscripts to the project that she's working on, and continue to take opportunities for writing to improve. She is at an appropriate level for all of these skills. I have no specific concerns.

feedback_value: You did a great job jumping into cross cover on a service with a fair amount of complexity. I observed on rounds that you filled in a few places on rounds, where the medical Student or Interns had questions and I could tell in the afternoon by the fact that everything was done in such an organized and clear fashion that you had led care all throughout the day. In reflecting on the day, I realize that I didn't sufficiently give you space to share thoughts on rounds for patients (although everything you did contribute was spot on). I apologize for that! If you notice this experience again on the sub specialties, I think it is great to discuss with the fellow/attending that you would like the opportunity to weigh in. You as the senior Resident will have the most general pediatrics knowledge often of anyone on the service. For example, you would be better equipped to think about constipation than I would! And, at the end of the day, much of what we deal with is general pediatrics. More importantly,, I will work on this for future senior residents that I work with as, ultimately, I do think the attending should be responsible for this and the Resident should not have to advocate! I also hate that I am dictating this without having had a chance to discuss it and happy to chat anytime at all!

activity_title: LEAD a Team and/or SUPERVISE others
instrument_title: Lead, supervise, and collaborate with other members of the healthcare team
activity_anchor: Lead a healthcare team (e.g., during rounds or an acute care event)
feedback_value: ****** led PHM rounds solo during our week together. She not only efficiently and safely directed patient care but she was able to lead the team through a particularly challenging encounter with a frustrated teenager with acute on chronic abdominal pain and few remaining treatment options. ****** prepared and encouraged the intern who felt empowered to lead off the discussion on rounds. ****** subsequently modeled for the team a productive and forward thinking discussion about principles of chronic pain management and relayed the difficult news that there were not additional options for management of chronic pain in the hospital and was able to lay out a plan for next steps to progress the patient towards home. The message was well received at the time but the patient subsequently became behaviorally disregulated after the team left the room, self-discontinuing her IV, and necessitating that the team call BRT. ***** debriefed with the team and they were able to finish rounds in a timely manner. I heard unsolicited feedback from the patient's family, our nurse practitioner who was in the room, and the intern on the team, and our students that *****'s counseling on pain and next steps could truly not have been better. She subsequently worked to continue to support the patient/family and organized a team meeting with all consulting teams later in the day. ******'s leadership in this situation was some of the strongest that I have seen by a resident in the **** in almost a decade. She approached a hard situation with care and calm, was able to offer attending level counseling on a complex interplay between mental health and chronic pain, and calmly supported the patient, family, and her interns and students after the conversation. Her thoughtful debrief afterward demonstrated a mature conceptualization of these situations as opportunities for personal and professional growth. I felt completely comfortable with her leading my team and the care of our patients independently.
supervision_anchor: RESIDENT LED CARE WITHOUT SUPERVISION NEEDED: May benefit from continued learning and growth; however, care would have been safe and effective without a supervisor

feedback_value: I was super appreciative to have the opportunity to work with ***** as the PHM supervisor this week he has many strengths, including his empathetic communication with families, his thoughtfulness and his medical savvy. ***** always had an appropriate thought process and plan. He also took ownership of tasks and communication for all of the patients on the team. Where ***** can improve as the team leader is making sure that there is an assessment stated in every room every day. Both of our interns skipped assessments, moving straight from updates to the plan which left families, confused and ultimately with questions that took a lot of time on Rounds, the interns expressed that they would like a chance to review their assessment with ***** either before rounds or before going in the room because this will make them feel more confident to present it. If ***** can check in very briefly with the interns before rounds just to make sure they share the same assessment This may ultimately lead to Rounds being much more efficient and leave time for more work and teaching for the rest of the day.

activity_title: Single Free Text Question- GENERAL FEEDBACK
assessment_type_anchor: Based on a single encounter or day
feedback_value: **** did a great job on her emergency shift recently. She is quick to pick up patients and is always asking to be helpful in anyway. Our presentations are concise and consistent. She has good intuition for disposition planning as well as formulating a management plan even in complex patients. Overall, **** was a pleasure to work with. In a single shift, it is difficult to give specific feedback for improvement, however, the pediatrics it's ultimately most important to rule out " cannot miss" diagnoses and can incorporate these in presentation and ultimately management plan. For instance. I don't think we need to do xyz because of xyz. This comes with experience and of course, learning through reading and seeing more patients. Continue to stay enthusiastic and the more patience you see the more you learn! I hope to work again with **** in the future.
next_level_value: Double response- I think I answered this one In The other response woops

activity_title: Single Free Text Question- COMMUNICATING WITH PATIENT AND FAMILIES/PROVIDING FAMILY-CENTERED CARE
assessment_type_anchor: Based on a single encounter or day
family_centered_care_value: ****** did a phenomenal job communicating with a family that refused vitamin K. She sat down with them on multiple days to address their concerns. She addressed their distress on how to interpret the information they read and determine credible sources and guided them to thinking about different types of information. ****** approached the conversations without judgement, allowing the family to feel safe being vulnerable and open up about their concerns. ******'s comfort with these conversations and eagerness to engage with patients in this way is admirable.

activity_title: Single Free Text Question- GENERAL FEEDBACK
feedback_value: ********* did a good job jumping in on the first week of her inpt rotation, especially in the setting of Epic rollout. she was open to feedback and eager to learn and improve. I like that she asked questions when she needed the help and should continue to do so as she builds up her skills intern year.
next_level_value: We discussed certain areas of improvement. For pre-rounding, discussed coming in earlier, looking over numbers/diagnostics over the last 24 hours, find a consistent stepwise approach to look for numbers/diagnostics, strategies to avoid getting stuck in rooms and what the goal of an efficient intern should be (finish pre-rounding at 8a, attend morning report and be ready to round by 830a). For rounding, we discussed what family centered rounds should look like, how to communicate with families, what numbers/diagnostics to present, how to present a clear assessment and plan. For post rounds, we discussed asking the senior to help with prioritization/assignment of tasks from rounds and new pages throughout the day so that important tasks are completed in a timely manner.

She also researched why a particular medication that was used off-label may have been effective, which may help her care for similar patients in the future.

next_level_value: Keep up the great work.

activity_title: ACUTE COMMON PROBLEM
feedback_value: For very complex patient with necrotizing pancreatitis and renal failure on dialysis, Dr. ****** appropriately synthesized all the data (lab, imaging, vitals, nursing, conflicting specialist guidance) and developed the assessments and plans each day, including contingency planning. Dr. ****** scrupulously followed up on every element of the decisions and care. Next steps: Each day, pretend that randomly one of the specialists (ID, renal dietician, hematologist, gynecologist, nephrologist, or pancreatologist) is busy with a crashing patient so they're not on site and you cannot get their advice. What would your plan be for that part? Then when you call or touch base with them, tell the specialist your preliminary idea of what to do and see if you're right! (As an attending, when I'm about to call a specialist I still do this, and put my idea or guess right after my question that I have for them, and the specialists seem to enjoy it, and the learning sticks better, especially when I get it wrong).

activity_title: ROUTINE HEALTH VISIT for child WITHOUT significant complex, chronic, or special health care needs
instrument_title: Providing preventative primary care for children of all ages
feedback_value: I think I had the honor of precepting **** for her first continuity patient at ***** ****. She did a great job of prepping to see the kid and her presentation was thorough without being overblown. She recognized both the importance of re-evaluating a previously documented cardiac murmur and the realistic challenge of actually hearing that murmur in a busy and resistant 12mo. She also had the insight to note that the organization of her presentation was a bit scattered (not awful, just not completely organized). I think that's a great goal for starting out, but overall, I was super-impressed with her first day!

activity_title: Single Free Text Question- PROFESSIONAL BEHAVIORS AND RESPONSIBILITIES
assessment_type_anchor: Based on a single encounter or day
professional_behaviors_and_responsibilit: Excited to have you on the team! It's clear you're genuinely enthusiastic about pediatrics, and you channel that excitement in such a professional way. I also noticed how respectful and thoughtful you were in your patient interactions - keep that up. Great start!

activity_title: ROUTINE HEALTH VISIT for child WITH complex, chronic, or special health care needs
instrument_title: Provide comprehensive healthcare for a child with complex, chronic, or special health care needs
feedback_value: While all comp care patients are required to be seen by the attending Dr ******** initiated the visit for the history and physical and then staffed and then completed the initial attempt of documentation. For her first week in internship I am incredibly impressed. Dr ******** showed a grasp of nuance and detail that many second or third year residents often struggle with. She was thorough, charismatic, professional and efficient. Well done.

activity_title: Single Free Text Question- COMMITMENT TO GROWTH AND LEARNING
assessment_type_anchor: Based on a single encounter or day
commitment_to_growth_and_learning_value: I really appreciated how committed you were to learning and trying out new tools. That kind of curiosity and openness will take you far. Keep playing around with these tools and working them into your regular flow. The more you use them, the more natural and helpful they'll feel in clinic

activity_title: ROUTINE HEALTH VISIT for child WITH complex, chronic, or special health care needs
instrument_title: Provide comprehensive healthcare for a child with complex, chronic, or special health care needs
feedback_value: This was a wellness visit for a teenage patient who had a history of anxiety, depression, and self harm and suicidal ideation several years ago. She's now is coming in for wellness care and has a return of some anxiety and depression and passive suicidal ideation. I think you did a great job with this visit drawing on some previously Learned tips from last week with a similar patient. It was nice to see you get the chance to practice a similar scenario with an adolescent who was endorsing thoughts of wishing they were dead, but not actively suicidal. You did a good job of bringing this up and giving the patient room to speak about her stressors. I think you did this in a very nonjudgmental way and the patient felt very comfortable talking to you about what is hard. There were some more complicated aspects of the visit such just starting a new medication for anxiety that we did together. I think you'll have more experience with that in the future and can work on talking to patients about side effects of medication and then also dosing options, how we titrate the medication based on patient response and arranging follow up care as needed. I think this visit showed great interval improvement around a sensitive topic in your ability to address this period with more exposure and conversations you'll be able to expand to providing the full spectrum of the discussion. Well done!
supervision_anchor: SUPERVISOR AND RESIDENT CO-LED CARE: Required supervising physician(s) to direct some aspects of the care

activity_title: ACUTE COMMON PROBLEM
feedback_value: This was a more complex diabetic patient that ****** admitted. She did a great job getting the details of the patient's history so that we could get an accurate representation of the patient! She asked great history questions and discussed the plan with the subspecialist to have their recommendations prior to presenting at night. ****** is learning about fluids and medications and how to appropriately dose in an adult sized child! Keep working on organizing your assessment and plan. As you continue to improve you can work to cut down on information and only include what is necessary to be more efficient in your presentations!

activity_title: ROUTINE HEALTH VISIT for child WITHOUT significant complex, chronic, or special health care needs
instrument_title: Providing preventative primary care for children of all ages
feedback_value: I think that this was ******'s first well child visit in the South Main system! There are a number of points for growth, especially related to developing independent scripting around key elements of the history and balancing thoroughness with efficiency. That can be really hard in the beginning. I'd recommend having a teaching resident or attending join you for your first few days in our clinic just to learn the flow of these exams. It will come! Your ability to bond with this adolescent around her experience as an immigrant to the U.S. was really wonderful and I could easily see how you had made her comfortable during the exam. Look forward to working with you again.

activity_title: ACUTE CARE VISIT NOT requiring transfer to higher level of care
instrument_title: Manage a patient with an acute, common diagnosis (Primary Care Clinic)
feedback_value: I thought you did a good job pre-charting and understanding the patient's background before going to visit them. I thought that it helped guide your discussions with them and helped give you context for what to do during the visit. So I would continue doing that as a lot of the patients that we see have many other providers. I also thought you created good rapport with our patients and their families in order for them to open up with you about mental health and their recreational activities. And you impressed a family enough who said that they were very happy with their care here in our clinic. Did a good job providing nutritional counseling and lifestyle changes to manage obesity and prediabetes. I would continue refining your assessment and plans and continue having patient centered goal building strategies for like weight loss and lifestyle changes. I think overall, you did very well with your counseling and it's just something that you will continue to hone

activity_title: Single Free Text Question- PROFESSIONAL BEHAVIORS AND RESPONSIBILITIES
professional_behaviors_and_responsibilit: Did an outstanding job maintaining professional behavior, upholding ethical standards, and always prioritizing patient care while she was taking care of a medically and socially complex patient. The patient was previously healthy and suffered an intracranial bleed with lots of neuro sequelae and behavioral problems. The patient required numerous code greens, attacked staff, and required many medications. The family was extremely difficulty, often times yelling at staff and becoming very stern/angry with the medical team while assigning unwarranted blame for things. The family was also very accusatory of events that were often false. Dr. ******* navigated this situation outstandingly. She maintained a calm demeanor, never got defensive, and was able to overcome many obstacles to continue to provide excellent care to the patient. Throughout the week, which was her first week on service caring for this patient, she was able to build rapport with the family, which many other providers have struggled to do.

activity_title: ACUTE COMMON PROBLEM
feedback_value: ***** and I saw an infant with prolonged fever and performed a Kawasaki work up. Before seeing the patient she correctly identified her differential for prolonged fever with only a few additions. She was very thoughtful with what exam findings were pertinent to rule out certain diagnoses on her differential (ie, normal neck range of motion, reassuring against meningitis). She did a good job using diagnostic tools such as the PECARN head injury rules. I was impressed with her eagerness to pick up patients and pop in on interesting patients that she was not following.

activity_title: Single Free Text Question- HIGH-VALUE CARE
providing_high_value_care_value: For one patient, Dr. ***** recognized the patient had been transferred to the OR without first receiving stress dose steroids. Dr. ***** immediately called the OR front desk and was able to relay the urgent need for steroid administration to the anesthesiologist. This prevented possible harm to this medically complex patient and was an example of high value and safe care. For another patient, Dr. ***** advocated for possibility of transfer to an outside facility closer to the patient's home. This is an example of high value care in multiple ways: it would allow the patient to be closer to his family and make it easier for the entire family to be together, it would also allow for the providers at the outside facility to get to know this patient and therefore hopefully prevent need for transfer to PCH with future illnesses. Though the patient will likely not transfer, exploring this option was very thoughtful practice on Dr. *****'s part.

You were able to recognize that the current concern did fall outside of the norm that we would expect for this age, but also balancing that with previous recommendations from the sub specialist. I think we came up with a good joint plan with Family and you assessed their level of comfort in a watching and waiting approach but also with appropriate guidelines for when to be more concerned that would necessitate sooner referral to ENT

knowing_limits_and_help_seeking_value: For a patient with diabetes, ready to come off the insulin drip and desperate and hungry to eat, but with an integrated insulin pump that was in Spanish language mode and lacking visible patient settings but primed and apparently ready to go, and nursing staff that was feeling uncertain, Dr ******* appropriately found a bilingual physician familiar with insulin pumps to review the settings on the device so that nurses could enter this on the appropriate form, let the child eat, and get him off the drip. This was not something an interpreter could have helped with. Dr ******* appropriately mitigated uncertainty before proceeding, which demonstrates her commitment to patient safety, health equity for patients who prefer language other than English, and to collegiality with nurses.

activity_title: Single Free Text Question- COMMUNICATING WITH PATIENT AND FAMILIES/PROVIDING FAMILY-CENTERED CARE
family_centered_care_value: Dr ********* did an excellent job throughout the week of ***** communicating with his patients and families. Below are several examples. 1. A leukemia patient with significant neurological dysfunction who had repeated setbacks in achieving discharge to attend an important life event. Dr ********* provided empathetic listening to the patient and his mother. He validated their feelings of fear, sadness and frustration. He also heard their concerns about discharge and made sure the family was not sent home when they were not ready. He also was a conduit to communication between multiple subspecialists involved in the patients care. 2. A patient with a new concern for cancer whose parents do not speak English. Dr. ********* communicated complex topics via a iPad translator. He answered the mother's questions until she understood the rationale for procedures and offered to have the surgery team return to answer additional questions. 3. Dr. ********* talked through the workup for a new cancer diagnosis patient with empathy and kindness.

activity_title: Promoting Equitable Care

feedback_value: Did an outstanding job helping coordinate outpatient resources for a patient with a complicated eating disorder. She went above and beyond to help coordinate outpatient follow up, which included referral to adolescent medicine. She also set up a follow appointment with herself in her own clinic, as she was the provider caring for this patient while he was hospitalized.

Thank you did a good job of getting the patients perspective for how their school year was going and how they felt about their mood. Patient is generally very shy and I think you made them at ease. I think you did a good job of framing the discussion around the possibility of weaning back medications. I think in the future, you could start with talking about weaning versus discontinuation first. I do think all of this was covered in the visit, but may have been a more gradual step into the discussion. You did a good job affirming various ways people use these medications for short periods of time or more prolonged periods. I think the family felt very empowered to do some follow up discussion at home and make a decision regarding what they feel is best for timing if they do want to wean medications

activity_title: Single Free Text Question- GENERAL FEEDBACK
feedback_value: ***** stated he was hoping to improve his detailedness from the last rotation. I think overall he did a great job with this! Good job improving!! One thing we discussed was that *****'s assessment and plans can get distracting. He often asks questions about seemingly random things or brings up esoteric diagnoses that then require a lot of explanation to families (and can cause distress to patients). Spending time on these irrelevant parts of the plan distracts the nurses, families and other team members from understanding goals of the day. I recommended he become more succinct (but maintain his detail oriented improved skills!). An example- we had a patient weaning to LFNC and in his plan he mentioned if she worsened, using TXA and intubating. She was on the improving phase and this was distracting and distressing. It would be relevant if she was escalating. Just because it's written on the handoff, doesn't mean in should be in the plan on rounds. Overall appreciated his hard work on our team!

activity_title: LONGITUDINAL CONTINUITY CLINIC ASSESSMENT
plan_anchor: The resident does not usually require help with the plan
supervision_anchor: Rarely or never
why_value: She is consistently respectful of patients and families, and her attention to detail never overshadows her ability to see the whole clinical picture. One of her greatest strengths is recognizing and responding to family dynamics and cultural sensitivities, which adds depth to her care. She demonstrates strong professional boundaries and models this well for others. She is remarkably willing to step in and help-sometimes almost too willing. That generosity is a gift to the team, though she should remain mindful of balancing it to avoid burnout. She has a natural ability to pick up on the nuances of a situation quickly and apply them thoughtfully in her care. What makes her stand out is the energy she brings to patient care-it's contagious and lifts the team. She already knows she wants to land in heme/onc, and it is a perfect fit for her skills and temperament. The combination of her empathy, adaptability, and ability to connect deeply with patients will serve her well in such a challenging and meaningful field.

feedback_value: **** has done a fantastic job in the Pediatric ICU. He was tasked with taking care of and coordinating care for the most complex patient on our unit and did a wonderful job. He's dedicated to his patients, pleasant to work with and will make an excellent consultant in the future.
next_level_value: This is very, very hard to do as a resident, but beginning to look at the evidence behind decision making and being able to cite papers as part of your medical reasoning is a super next level skill. It's something that some of my top attendings do and will increase your credibility a ton.

What could be developed is keeping an organized flow even when a visit seems like it might need to go to the ED- Dr ******* knew based on data (temp) that this infant needed to be transferred, but came to staff before examining them; it is good practice to be thorough and collect all the data before moving to the next step so we don't miss something.

feedback_value: *** is the most exceptional senior resident I have had the opportunity to work with over the past 13 months on the ******** team. I have done 13 weeks of service during this timeframe. During the week, we had the most ******** patients I have ever had on service (7), all of which were complex -- single ventricle patients, arrhythmia patients. She did an exceptional job leading her team and supervising her junior residents. This week occurred on the first week of EPIC roll out. She excelled in helping to discharge patients in a timely manner and arrange for home oxygen/home care. She worked within the system and was creative in coming up with problem solving strategies to coordinate patient needs (arranging home oxygen by signing paper orders). She additionally personally ensured that all medication reconciliations were done so that there were NO errors in discharge medication. This was reflected in the discharge paperwork and the medications family picked up from pharmacy -- this is a huge deal and ensured that patients were discharged safely. She had challenging residents who were new to wards and who were struggling and needed a lot of support, patience, kindness and time. *** recognized these limitations and did everything possible to keep all patients safe all week and support and help the residents continuously improve from week to week. Residents felt supported. She has an excellent CAN DO attitude and is willing to work hard and stay late and provide the best possible care to her patients and families while being kind and professional at all times despite challenges and frustrations. She is for sure a role model to her junior residents and I would argue for me as how to keep calm, get the work done and be kind while doing it. She is an amazing colleague and I am so happy that I had the opportunity to work with her and there is no way that I would have been able to get through this week without her excellent patient care and coordination.

activity_title: HANDOVER to and from on admission, transfer, discharge, and care transitions
instrument_title: Facilitate a handover to and from other health care providers (either within or across settings)
feedback_value: Nice job collecting and conveying history in a time-pressure setting of later admissions. In documentation, try to include at least 3-5 ROS that are pertinent to the problem (if not all!). This is your opportunity to find things parents might not have mentioned or additional clues that you might not have picked up that alter the diagnosis. Also, work on typing in PMH and family hx as the ones that pull in aren't always accurate and dont easily show up in the H&P (an error of our template). Keep expanding your problem list and think about the differential for each one. A stated Benadryl ingestion could be a coingestion, if there is an AKI, list that as another problem with a differential and next steps plus when to recheck. Building a plan independently is a tough thing to learn. I tend to start by writing down not only the admitting problem but other abnormalities - either PMH that we need to consider (risk for aspiration), abnormal labs (AGMA), or physical exam findings. Then you can discuss that you think the AGMA is from dehydration and will repeat labs in 4h but if not improved, may consider additional testing. If we are thinking about how a prior problem is contributing to their hospital stay, or giving a medicine, go ahead and list it! Keep up the good work!

activity_title: CONSULT a subspecialist/surgeon- identification of need, communicate question, follow up recommendations
instrument_title: Seek and manage consultation or referral for patients who require subspecialty medical or surgical care
feedback_value: ****** did a great job taking the lead on consulting ID for a newborn with concern for syphilis management. Even with some technical difficulties, he navigated the conversation well and knew the patient details well. He effectively communicated the reason for the consult. Fortunately this was a fairly straightforward consult, but many others in the future may not be. Some helpful points for future consults: 1) before calling, write out your question and any necessary details or dates. ID especially loves a detailed story so have that ready to go with your thoughts organized. 2) introduce yourself and what your role is. 3) start with a little one liner for what the question is ("I'm calling for guidance on congenital syphilis management") before launching into the story. And always ask at the end what their recs are for follow up. Just a few pointers to start with.

activity_title: LONGITUDINAL CONTINUITY CLINIC ASSESSMENT
plan_anchor: The resident requires a little help with the plan
supervision_anchor: Less than half the time
why_value: **** was able to come up with a good differential with top diagnosis based on history and physical for the acute care patients he saw. He was thorough with history taking and documented this well. Did still need a little help with the plan for certain patients and explaining this to family, though did do a good job of contingency planning with family should symptoms not improve. Would caution you on use of dot phrase physical exams that are overly thorough as you may need to delete things that you didn't do for certain visits that don't require as thorough of a physical exam. For example - full abdominal exam including auscultation on a kid who was here for rash? GU exam was documented for a kid who was here for healthy habits follow up? I have a more brief physical exam that is limited to heart, lungs, and some general things you can observe that I will use for straightforward visits that tends to be more useful and if you do extra steps you can add those in rather than having to ensure you take out what you did not do. Hope this makes sense - happy to discuss more when we work together next!

feedback_value: Dr **** provided exceptional care to this complicated patient. She was well-prepared for the visit and came in with an agenda for what to discuss. This increased her efficiency with the visit, and made sure work was not being duplicated. She addressed all concerns from the family, and they felt listened to and like their needs were met.
supervision_anchor: RESIDENT LED CARE WITHOUT SUPERVISION NEEDED: May benefit from continued learning and growth; however, care would have been safe and effective without a supervisor

activity_title: LONGITUDINAL CONTINUITY CLINIC ASSESSMENT
plan_anchor: The resident requires a little help with the plan
supervision_anchor: Less than half the time
why_value: **** does an excellent job of gathering a detailed history, coming up with a broad but appropriate differential and identifying a plan to help rule in or out various diagnoses or treat conditions. She saw several varying sick visits and did a great job addressing patient concerns and knew her limits and when to ask for help (although most of the time her instincts and tentative plan were already right on track!). I think she is nearly ready for independent practice and will make a great general pediatrician. Keep working on confidence, but never be afraid to ask for help if needed! Also documentation is consistently very thorough yet concise. Perfect balance.

activity_title: LONGITUDINAL CONTINUITY CLINIC ASSESSMENT
plan_anchor: The resident requires a little help with the plan
supervision_anchor: Less than half the time
why_value: ***** continues to be a thorough resident and does a great job in both acute care visits as well as well checks, even those that become more complex with added issues that need to be addressed. She typically comes up with plans on her own which occasionally need small tweaking and she has good insight to where her limitations are and knows when to ask for help or look things up which is a very important skill. She is efficient while still remaining through and has great bedside manner. Her documentation is excellent and usually requires little modification. If she continues to grow as she has over the last year I have no doubt she will be ready for independent practice by the end of the year.
